findings.reachability: Any caller passing attacker-controlled key material to importKey() without specifying a format triggers autoImport, which tries each format's regex in sequence. The regexes are applied to the full string before any structural parsing, so a crafted ~50 kB string with no END marker causes multi-second hangs.
findings.justification: All four autoImport functions use regexes of the form /^[\S\s]*-----BEGIN ...-----\s*(?=(([A-Za-z0-9+/=]+\s*)+))\1-----END ...-----[\S\s]*$/g — the inner group ([A-Za-z0-9+/=]+\s*)+ with nested quantifiers plus a backreference inside a lookahead causes exponential backtracking when the END delimiter is absent or misplaced.

detection_pattern.section_name: ReDoS: nested quantifiers in PEM autodetect regex
detection_pattern.description: Catches nested-quantifier patterns used for PEM/SSH key format autodetection; exponential backtracking occurs when the input lacks the closing delimiter.

findings.location: PssScheme.hash() line 3126 (DEFAULT_HASH3 = 'sha1'), NodeNativePssScheme.sign/verify lines 4177/4189 (DEFAULT_PSS_HASH = 'sha1'), OaepScheme.hash() line 2877 (DEFAULT_HASH = 'sha1')
findings.reachability: Affects all callers that construct NodeRSA with the default signingScheme ('pss') or encryptionScheme ('pkcs1_oaep') without explicitly setting a hash option. This is the common out-of-the-box usage.
findings.justification: The PSS signing scheme defaults to SHA-1 (cryptographically deprecated for signatures since 2011; NIST SP 800-131A requires SHA-2 for new systems). OAEP also defaults to SHA-1 for the hash and MGF, weakening ciphertext indistinguishability against future SHA-1 collision-class attacks.

detection_pattern.section_name: Weak default: SHA-1 as default hash
detection_pattern.description: Identifies packages that hard-code SHA-1 as the default hash for signing or OAEP — callers who omit the hash option silently use a deprecated algorithm.

findings.reachability: Reached when a caller invokes decrypt(ciphertext, 'json') and the decrypted plaintext is not valid JSON. This can occur with wrong keys, corrupted ciphertexts, or when an adversary controls the ciphertext in a scenario where decryption succeeds but payload is crafted.
findings.justification: Line 3887 calls JSON.parse(toUtf8(bytes)) with no surrounding try/catch; a SyntaxError propagates through the catch-all in $$decryptKey (line 3871-3873) which rethrows as 'Error during decryption', leaking that the ciphertext decrypted successfully but payload is malformed — a mild oracle distinguishable from a true decryption failure.

detection_pattern.description: Flags bare JSON.parse calls not wrapped in try/catch; thrown SyntaxErrors can crash callers or leak information about successful decryption when used in crypto decode paths.

findings.location: JsEngine.decrypt() lines 3393-3414, NodeNativeEngine.decrypt() lines 4026-4041 (nativeAvailableForDecrypt returns false for pkcs1 private path, routing to JS fallback)
findings.reachability: Callers that configure encryptionScheme:'pkcs1' and expose a decryption endpoint over a network where an attacker can submit chosen ciphertexts and observe whether decryption succeeds or fails (binary oracle). The code itself documents this at line 3024-3033.
findings.justification: The code comment at lines 3024-3033 explicitly acknowledges that while internal differential timing is mitigated, 'the valid/invalid binary oracle inherent to PKCS#1 v1.5 remains' — the JsEngine.decrypt() path throws 'Decryption failed' on any padding error, making success vs. failure distinguishable, enabling a Bleichenbacher attack to decrypt arbitrary RSA-PKCS1v1.5 ciphertexts in ~10^6 queries.

detection_pattern.section_name: PKCS#1 v1.5 binary oracle: distinguishable decryption failure
detection_pattern.description: Flags PKCS#1 v1.5 decryption paths that throw or return distinct errors on padding failures; enables Bleichenbacher-style oracle attacks if the decryption endpoint is network-accessible.
